claim_1_genuine_utility.evidence: Of 18/20 recent L-601 citations: 4 PROCEDURAL, 2 BOTH, 1 CONCEPTUAL, 11 UNCLEAR (header-only). 61% of citations show no substantive engagement with L-601 concept. Citation is reflexive/procedural, not conceptual dependency.
claim_1_genuine_utility.conclusion: L-601 in-degree is measurement-inflated by ~60%. Effective conceptual in-degree is ~168 (40% of 421), still highest but dominance ratio drops to ~3x

claim_2_no_fragmentation.evidence: 99.1% connectivity after L-601 removal. Only 11/1220 lessons disconnected.
claim_2_no_fragmentation.conclusion: Graph is structurally robust to hub removal. L-601 monopoly is not a fragmentation risk.

claim_3_decelerating.conclusion: Natural saturation effect. L-601 citation rate declining as newer lessons have weaker procedural connections.

analysis.overall_verdict: PARTIALLY FALSIFIED: L-601 monopoly is NOT a structural fragmentation risk (graph robust), but citation rate IS measurement-inflated by ~60% procedural/reflexive citations. The 35% threshold is not structurally meaningful because most citations are shallow. Real concern: citation inflation masks actual knowledge structure.
analysis.new_finding: Citation inflation pattern: high-in-degree lessons accumulate reflexive citations (Cites: header listings) that dont reflect conceptual dependency. This inflates apparent K_avg and hub metrics. Effective K_avg (conceptual only) would be ~40% lower.

effect_size.procedural_inflation: ~60% of recent L-601 citations are non-substantive
